summary: DHH explains why he stopped doing full-system backups, instead treating every computer as a stateless, disposable unit. By storing all important data in Dropbox and code in Git, he maintains distributed local copies across multiple machines. This approach means any single machine can be lost or destroyed without consequence. He extended this philosophy to create Omarchy, encoding his entire system configuration into an ISO that installs in two minutes.
key_points: Every machine should be treated as a stateless, disposable unit with full-disk encryption
Dropbox and Git provide distributed local copies — not just cloud storage — so you're protected even if the services go offline
The discipline required is routing all important data through Dropbox; everything outside it is considered temporary and disposable
System configuration should be codified too, which motivated the creation of Omarchy as a two-minute install ISO
money_quotes: Every machine I now own is treated as a stateless, disposable unit that can be stolen, lost, or corrupted without consequences.
The combination of full-disk encryption and distributed copies of all important data means there's just no stress if anything bad happens to the computer.
But don't mistake this for just a 'everything is in the cloud' argument.
Everything outside of Dropbox is essentially treated as a temporary directory that's fully disposable.
It seemed so unreasonable that the configuration needed for a fully functional system still took hours on end.
There ought to be no reason why a busted computer is a calamity of data loss or a long restore process.
key_insight: By treating every computer as disposable and routing all important data through distributed sync services, you can eliminate the need for traditional backups entirely.
